walkthrough.title: Choose a Race Course
walkthrough.text: Alright, time to set up some robot chaos! First, pick a race course. You will see double-sided course boards in the box. For your first game, use the Dizzy Highway side. It is the simplest course and great for learning. Connect the course board to the start board so the arrows on the start board point toward the course.
walkthrough.image_caption: Board elements reference card showing all course features

walkthrough.title: Place the Checkpoint Flags
walkthrough.text: Now grab the checkpoint tokens. These are the numbered green translucent flags and matching square tiles. Look at your chosen course and find the checkpoint spaces marked on the board. Place the checkpoint flags in numerical order, starting with number one. On Dizzy Highway you will use checkpoints one and two. Other courses may use up to six checkpoints. These are the finish line goals your robot needs to race through in order.
walkthrough.image_caption: Numbered checkpoint flags and tiles, one through six

walkthrough.title: Place the Priority Antenna
walkthrough.text: Find the round gray priority antenna token. It has a little antenna icon with signal waves on it. Place it on the marked priority antenna space on the course board. This antenna determines turn order every single register. The robot closest to the antenna goes first. It is one of the most important things on the board, so make sure everyone can see it.
walkthrough.image_caption: Priority antenna token — determines who moves first each register

walkthrough.title: Place Reboot Tokens
walkthrough.text: Now find the green reboot tokens. These are square tiles with a circular refresh arrow icon on them. Place one reboot token on each reboot space marked on the course board. There is also one on the start board. When a robot gets destroyed or falls off the board, it will respawn on the nearest reboot token. Think of them as your robot's save points.
walkthrough.image_caption: Green reboot tokens with refresh arrow icons

walkthrough.title: Pick Your Robot
walkthrough.text: Here is the fun part. There are six awesome pre-painted robot miniatures to choose from. You have got Spin Bot, Zoom Bot, Hammer Bot, Twonky, Smash Bot, and Hulk X ninety. Each one looks totally different but they all play the same way. Pick whichever robot speaks to you. Maybe you like Twonky's boxy look, or maybe Hulk X ninety's heavy metal vibe is more your style.
walkthrough.image_caption: All six pre-painted robot miniatures

walkthrough.title: Grab Your Player Mat and Cards
walkthrough.text: Once you have picked your robot, take the matching player mat. Your mat has slots for everything. On the left side you will see the Draw pile and Discard pile areas. Along the bottom are five numbered register slots where you will place your programming cards each round. On the right side is your energy tracker going from zero to ten, and your checkpoint tracker. Also grab your matching robot identity card and priority token.
walkthrough.image_caption: Player mat showing registers, energy tracker, and card slots

walkthrough.title: Shuffle Your Programming Deck
walkthrough.text: Each player gets their own personal programming deck of twenty cards. These are the cards you will use to program your robot's movements each round. Give your deck a good shuffle and place it face down on the Draw slot on your player mat. Do not look at the cards yet. You will draw from this deck at the start of each Programming Phase.
walkthrough.image_caption: Robot identity cards for all six robots

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Energy Supply
walkthrough.text: Now for the energy cubes. These are the small translucent orange cubes. Each player starts with five energy cubes. Place them on or near your player mat and set your energy tracker to five. Put all the remaining energy cubes in a central pile that everyone can reach. This is the energy bank. You will gain and spend energy cubes throughout the game to buy powerful upgrades for your robot.
walkthrough.image_caption: Translucent orange energy cubes

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Upgrade Shop
walkthrough.text: Find the upgrade card deck. These are special ability cards your robot can install during the Upgrade Phase. Shuffle them into a face-down deck. Then deal face-up upgrade cards equal to the number of players. So in a four player game, deal four cards face up. This is the upgrade shop. Players will be able to browse and buy from this shop at the start of each round.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Damage Decks
walkthrough.text: Separate the four types of damage cards into their own face-up piles near the board. You have SPAM damage, which is the most common. Worm damage, which forces a reboot. Trojan Horse damage, which gives you bonus SPAM on top. And Virus damage, which spreads to nearby robots. Keep these piles within reach because robots will be taking damage all game long. That is just how factory floors work.

walkthrough.title: Set Out Special Programming Cards
walkthrough.text: Place the six special programming cards face up near the board where everyone can see them. These are Energy Routine, Weasel Routine, Spam Folder, Sandbox Routine, Speed Routine, and Repeat Routine. These cards are not in your programming deck. Instead, they become available through certain game effects. Think of them as bonus moves your robot can learn.

walkthrough.title: Place Robots on the Start Board
walkthrough.text: Almost there! The youngest player places their robot first on any black and white gear space on the start board, pointing the robot toward the course. Then placement continues to the left, with each player picking an open gear space. Your robot must face toward the course. Once everyone is placed, grab the sand timer and put it within reach. You are ready to race! Let's learn how to program these bots.
walkthrough.image_caption: Priority tokens in each player color

summary.title: Choose a Race Course
bullets: Pick a course board (Dizzy Highway for beginners)
Connect course board to the start board with arrows pointing toward course

summary.title: Place the Checkpoint Flags
bullets: Place numbered checkpoint flags on marked spaces in order (one through six)
Dizzy Highway uses checkpoints one and two

summary.title: Place the Priority Antenna
bullets: Place round antenna token on its marked board space
Determines turn order — closest robot to antenna has priority

summary.title: Place Reboot Tokens
bullets: Place green reboot tokens on marked reboot spaces
Destroyed robots respawn on the nearest reboot token

summary.title: Pick Your Robot
bullets: Six robots: Spin Bot, Zoom Bot, Hammer Bot, Twonky, Smash Bot, Hulk X90
All play identically — pick your favorite

summary.title: Grab Your Player Mat and Cards
bullets: Take matching player mat, robot identity card, and priority token
Mat has: Draw pile, Discard pile, five register slots, energy tracker, checkpoint tracker

summary.title: Shuffle Your Programming Deck
bullets: Each player gets a personal twenty card programming deck
Shuffle and place face-down on the Draw slot on your player mat

summary.title: Set Up the Energy Supply
bullets: Each player starts with five energy cubes, tracker set to five
Remaining cubes form a central bank

summary.title: Set Up the Upgrade Shop
bullets: Shuffle upgrade deck, deal face-up cards equal to player count
This forms the upgrade shop for purchasing each round

summary.title: Prepare the Damage Decks
bullets: Separate four damage types into face-up piles: SPAM, Worm, Trojan Horse, Virus
Keep near board — robots will take damage frequently

summary.title: Set Out Special Programming Cards
bullets: Place six special cards face-up near board
Energy Routine, Weasel Routine, Spam Folder, Sandbox Routine, Speed Routine, Repeat Routine

summary.title: Place Robots on the Start Board
bullets: Youngest player places first on any start board gear space
Placement continues to the left, robots face toward course
Place sand timer within reach

walkthrough.title: The Four Phases of a Round
walkthrough.text: Alright, time to program some chaos! Each round your robot is going to zoom, crash, and laser its way across the factory floor. Every round has four phases that happen in the same order. Phase one is the Upgrade Phase, where you can buy cool upgrades. Phase two is the Programming Phase, where you secretly choose your robot's next five moves. Phase three is the Activation Phase, where everything goes wild as robots move and board elements fire. Phase four is the End of Round cleanup. Let's break each one down.
walkthrough.image_caption: Round Sequence reference card showing all four phases

walkthrough.title: Phase One: Upgrade
walkthrough.text: At the start of each round, you get a chance to upgrade your robot. Going in priority order, which means closest to the antenna goes first, each player may spend energy cubes to buy one upgrade card from the shop. There are two types of upgrades. Permanent upgrades have a yellow border and stay installed for the entire game. Temporary upgrades have a red border and are one-time use. You can have up to three of each type installed on your mat at a time. If you are full, you can discard one to make room. After everyone has had a chance to buy, discard the remaining shop cards and deal new ones equal to the player count.

walkthrough.title: Phase Two: Programming
walkthrough.text: Now for the heart of the game. Everyone programs their robot at the same time. Draw nine cards from your personal programming deck. Look at them carefully, then choose five cards and place them face down on your five register slots, in order from register one through five. These are your robot's next five moves and they will execute in that exact order, no take-backs! Discard the four cards you did not use. Here is the pressure: once the first player finishes programming, flip the sand timer. Everyone else has thirty seconds to finish or their remaining registers get filled with random cards from their deck.

walkthrough.title: Programming Cards: Movement
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about what cards you can program. The movement cards are Move one, Move two, and Move three. These move your robot forward that many spaces in the direction it is currently facing. If your robot runs into another robot, it pushes that robot one space in the same direction. Pushing chains can happen too, so one robot can shove a whole line of bots. Then there is Back Up, which moves your robot one space backward without changing which direction it faces. Very handy for avoiding pits.

walkthrough.title: Programming Cards: Turning
walkthrough.text: Turn Left rotates your robot ninety degrees to the left. Turn Right rotates ninety degrees to the right. And U-Turn flips your robot one hundred eighty degrees so it faces the complete opposite direction. These are crucial because your robot always moves in the direction it is facing. If you are pointed at a wall, a Move three card sends you crashing right into it. Plan your turns carefully.

walkthrough.title: Programming Cards: Power Up and Again
walkthrough.text: Two more card types round out your programming options. Power Up does not move your robot at all. Instead, you collect one energy cube from the bank and add it to your supply. Energy is valuable because it buys upgrades, so sometimes standing still is the smart play. Then there is Again, which repeats whatever your previous register did. So if register three was a Move two, and register four is Again, your robot moves two spaces forward again. If Again is in register one, it does nothing because there is no previous register to copy.

walkthrough.title: Phase Three: Activation Overview
walkthrough.text: This is where the magic and mayhem happen. The Activation Phase resolves your five registers one at a time, from register one through five. For each register, first all robots reveal their programmed card. Then robots execute their cards in priority order, meaning the robot closest to the priority antenna goes first, then the next closest, and so on. After all robots have moved for that register, the board elements activate in a specific order. Then you move to the next register and repeat. Five registers total, each one a mini round of chaos.

walkthrough.title: Priority Order
walkthrough.text: Matters a lot because moving first means you might push someone off course before they get to move. To determine priority, count the number of spaces between each robot and the priority antenna. Count by the shortest path using rows and columns, not diagonals. The closest robot has highest priority and moves first. If two robots are tied in distance, imagine a line extending from the antenna's dish and sweeping clockwise. Whichever robot the line hits first has priority.
walkthrough.image_caption: Priority antenna — closest robot moves first

walkthrough.title: Board Elements: Conveyor Belts
walkthrough.text: After all robots execute their programmed card for a register, the board elements activate in a strict order. First up are blue conveyor belts. These are the fast ones. They move any robot standing on them two spaces in the direction of the arrows. If a conveyor turns, the robot turns with it. Next are green conveyor belts. These are slower, moving robots just one space in the arrow direction. Conveyors can chain together, carrying robots across multiple belt segments. But if a conveyor would push a robot into another robot that is not also on a conveyor, the push does not happen.
walkthrough.image_caption: Board Elements reference showing conveyor belts, gears, lasers, and more

walkthrough.title: Board Elements: Push Panels and Gears
walkthrough.text: After conveyors, push panels activate. These only fire during specific numbered registers, shown on the panel. For example, a push panel marked with a two and four only activates during registers two and four. When active, they shove any robot on them one space in the direction the panel faces. Next come gears. Gears rotate any robot standing on them ninety degrees in the direction shown by the arrow on the gear. Clockwise gears turn you right, counterclockwise gears turn you left.

walkthrough.title: Board Elements: Lasers
walkthrough.text: After gears, the board's laser beams fire. These are the red lines printed on the board, shooting from laser emitters along walls. If a robot is in the path of a board laser, it takes one SPAM damage card. Lasers are blocked by walls and by other robots, so hiding behind a friend is a legitimate strategy. Right after board lasers, each robot fires its own laser. Your robot shoots a beam straight ahead in the direction it faces. If another robot is in the line of fire without a wall blocking it, that robot takes one SPAM damage card.

walkthrough.title: Board Elements: Energy Spaces and Checkpoints
walkthrough.text: After lasers, energy spaces activate. If your robot is standing on an energy space and there is still an energy cube on it, you collect that cube. Energy spaces refill at specific times. The important note is that if you end register five on an energy space, you always collect one energy cube from the bank, even if the space was already empty. Finally, checkpoints are checked. If your robot is on a checkpoint and it is the next one in your sequence, you claim it and advance your checkpoint tracker. You must claim them in order, one, two, three, and so on.

walkthrough.title: Damage: SPAM Cards
walkthrough.text: Now let's talk about getting hurt, because your robot is going to take a lot of damage. The most common damage type is SPAM. When you take SPAM damage, you draw a SPAM card from the damage pile and put it into your discard pile. The nasty thing about SPAM is that it pollutes your programming deck. When you later shuffle your discard pile to form a new draw deck, those SPAM cards are mixed in. If you draw a SPAM card during programming, you must place it face up on a register. When that register activates, you discard the SPAM and draw the top card of your deck, executing whatever it is. Total chaos.

walkthrough.title: Damage: Worm, Trojan Horse, and Virus
walkthrough.text: The other three damage types are nastier. A Worm card works like SPAM in your deck, but when you program it into a register and it activates, your robot immediately reboots. Ouch. A Trojan Horse card also goes in your deck, but when it activates, you take two additional SPAM cards on top of discarding the Trojan Horse. Double ouch. And the worst one, a Virus card, when it activates, every robot within a six space radius of you also takes a Virus card. It literally infects nearby robots. Keep your distance from a glitching bot.

walkthrough.title: Rebooting
walkthrough.text: Sometimes your robot gets destroyed. This happens if you fall off the edge of the board, land in a pit, or trigger a Worm card. When you reboot, several things happen. First, you take two SPAM damage cards. Second, you discard all of your remaining programming cards for this round. Third, place your robot on the nearest reboot token, choosing any direction to face. You can still be shot or pushed by other robots while on the reboot token, but you cannot shoot anyone. Your remaining registers for this round are skipped. Next round you start fresh from the reboot space.
walkthrough.image_caption: Reboot tokens — your robot's respawn points

walkthrough.title: Robot Interactions: Pushing
walkthrough.text: Robots interact with each other constantly. The biggest interaction is pushing. When your robot moves into a space occupied by another robot, you push that robot one space in your direction of movement. If that pushed robot hits another robot, it pushes that one too, creating a chain. You cannot push robots through walls. If a wall blocks the push, your robot simply stops. But here is the fun part: you can absolutely push robots into pits, off the board edge, or onto conveyor belts that carry them somewhere terrible. Positioning is everything.

walkthrough.title: Walls and Line of Sight
walkthrough.text: Walls are printed on the edges of board spaces. They block robot movement, pushes, and laser fire. A robot cannot move through a wall, period. If a programming card would move you into a wall, you just stop. Walls only block from the side they are on, so you can move freely from the other direction. For laser line of sight, draw an imaginary line from the robot or laser emitter straight ahead. It stops at the first wall or robot it hits. Board lasers and robot lasers both follow these same rules.

walkthrough.title: Phase Four: End of Round
walkthrough.text: After all five registers have been resolved, the round is over. Clean up time. All players move their programming cards from the registers to their discard pile. If your draw pile is empty next round, you will shuffle your discard pile, including any SPAM and damage cards mixed in, to form a new draw pile. The upgrade shop gets refreshed with new face-up cards equal to the player count. Then start the next round from Phase one, the Upgrade Phase. The race continues until someone crosses that final checkpoint.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Race
walkthrough.text: The goal is simple: be the first robot to reach all the checkpoints in order. You must hit checkpoint one before checkpoint two, two before three, and so on. You claim a checkpoint at the end of a register when you are standing on it and it is the next one in your sequence. The game ends immediately when any player claims the final checkpoint on the course. That player wins! It does not matter how banged up your robot is or how many SPAM cards are clogging your deck. Cross that finish line first and you are the champion of the factory floor.

summary.title: The Four Phases of a Round
bullets: Phase one: Upgrade — buy robot upgrades with energy
Phase two: Programming — draw nine cards, pick five for registers
Phase three: Activation — resolve registers one through five with board elements
Phase four: End of Round — discard programming cards, refresh upgrade shop

summary.title: Phase One: Upgrade
bullets: Priority order (closest to antenna goes first)
Spend energy to buy one upgrade from the shop
Permanent (yellow, lasts all game) or temporary (red, one use)
Maximum three of each type installed at once

summary.title: Phase Two: Programming
bullets: Everyone programs simultaneously
Draw nine cards, pick five, place face-down on registers one through five
Discard remaining four cards
Thirty second timer starts when first player finishes

summary.title: Programming Cards: Movement
bullets: Move one, two, or three: advance forward that many spaces
Back Up: move one space backward, no turning
Pushing: running into a robot pushes it (and any chain behind it)

summary.title: Programming Cards: Turning
bullets: Turn Left or Right: rotate ninety degrees in place
U-Turn: rotate one hundred eighty degrees
Robot always moves in the direction it faces

summary.title: Programming Cards: Power Up and Again
bullets: Power Up: gain one energy cube, robot stays in place
Again: repeat previous register's action
Again in register one does nothing

summary.title: Phase Three: Activation Overview
bullets: Resolve registers one through five sequentially
Each register: reveal cards, execute in priority order, then board elements activate
Priority = closest to antenna moves first

summary.title: Priority Order
bullets: Count shortest path (rows and columns) from each robot to antenna
Closest robot has highest priority and moves first
Ties broken by clockwise sweep from antenna dish direction

summary.title: Board Elements: Conveyor Belts
bullets: Blue conveyors activate first — move robots two spaces in arrow direction
Green conveyors next — move robots one space in arrow direction
Conveyors chain together; robot turns with belt curves
Blocked if destination has non-conveyor robot

summary.title: Board Elements: Push Panels and Gears
bullets: Push panels: activate only on their numbered registers, push one space
Gears: rotate robot ninety degrees clockwise or counterclockwise

summary.title: Board Elements: Lasers
bullets: Board lasers fire — robots in path take one SPAM damage
Robot lasers fire — shoot straight ahead, first robot hit takes one SPAM
Both blocked by walls and other robots

summary.title: Board Elements: Energy Spaces and Checkpoints
bullets: Energy spaces: collect cube if available; always collect from bank on register five
Checkpoints: claim if it is your next in sequence (must go in order)
Advance checkpoint tracker when claimed

summary.title: Damage: SPAM Cards
bullets: SPAM goes into discard pile, pollutes your deck
When drawn during programming, place face-up on a register
On activation: discard SPAM, draw and execute top deck card instead

summary.title: Damage: Worm, Trojan Horse, and Virus
bullets: Worm: when programmed and activated, robot immediately reboots
Trojan Horse: when activated, take two additional SPAM cards
Virus: when activated, all robots within six spaces also take a Virus

summary.title: Rebooting
bullets: Triggered by: falling off board, entering pit, or Worm card
Take two SPAM cards, discard remaining programming
Place robot on nearest reboot token facing any direction
Skip remaining registers this round

summary.title: Robot Interactions: Pushing
bullets: Moving into another robot pushes it in your direction of movement
Push chains: pushed robot pushes the next robot, and so on
Cannot push through walls — movement stops
Can push robots into pits or off the board edge

bullets: Walls block movement, pushes, and laser fire
Walls are directional — only block from their side
Laser line of sight stops at first wall or robot

bullets: Move all register cards to discard pile
Empty draw pile? Shuffle discard (with damage cards) into new deck
Refresh upgrade shop with new cards equal to player count

Claim checkpoint at end of register when standing on it
Game ends immediately when any player claims the final checkpoint
That player wins the race

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Factory Floor
walkthrough.text: Your robots are about to crash into each other, get shoved by conveyor belts, and laser their friends. It is going to be glorious chaos — but there is a method to the madness. In this practice tutorial, we will walk through one complete round of Robo Rally with four players. Each player programs their robot differently, and one of them — Spin Bot — is going to learn some hard lessons the fun way. Follow along even if you have fewer players at your table. Ready? Robots, power up!
walkthrough.image_caption: All six robot miniatures ready for action

walkthrough.title: Meet the Robots
walkthrough.text: Here are our four players for this round. Player 1 is Zoom Bot, the green speedster — they are going to show clean, fundamental programming. Player 2 is Hammer Bot, the purple tactician — they will use conveyor belts strategically for free movement. Player 3 is Twonky, the orange aggressor — they are going after lasers and energy pickups. And Player 4 is Spin Bot, the blue newcomer — well, Spin Bot is going to learn by making mistakes. Three of them, actually. But that is how every robot operator learns.

walkthrough.title: The Board State
walkthrough.text: We are on Dizzy Highway, the beginner course. Checkpoint one is a few spaces ahead with some conveyor belts in between. There is a priority antenna on the board, a couple of board lasers, and some energy spaces scattered around. All four robots start on the start board, facing toward the course. Everyone draws their nine programming cards and it is time to plan five moves.
walkthrough.image_caption: Dizzy Highway board with conveyor belts, lasers, and checkpoints

walkthrough.title: Programming Phase: Everyone Plans
walkthrough.text: Remember, everyone programs at the same time. You draw nine cards from your personal deck and pick five to place on your registers in order, one through five. The other four cards get discarded. Think of it like writing a five-step dance routine for your robot. Let's see what each player decides.

walkthrough.title: Zoom Bot's Program (Clean Play)
walkthrough.text: Player 1, Zoom Bot, looks at the board carefully. Checkpoint one is ahead and slightly to the right. Zoom Bot programs: Register 1 — Move 1. Register 2 — Move 2. Register 3 — Turn Right. Register 4 — Move 1. Register 5 — Move 1. Zoom Bot traced the full path with their finger before placing any cards. They know exactly where their robot will be after each register. This is textbook programming.

walkthrough.title: Why Tracing Your Path Works
walkthrough.text: Before Zoom Bot placed a single card, they pointed at the board and traced every step. After Move 1 — here. After Move 2 — here. Turn Right — now facing this way. Move 1 — here. Move 1 — lands right next to the checkpoint. No walls in the way, no pits, no surprises. This is the single most important habit in Robo Rally. Trace your path with your finger before you lock in. Every time.

walkthrough.title: Hammer Bot's Program (Conveyor Strategy)
walkthrough.text: Player 2, Hammer Bot, spots a blue conveyor belt two spaces ahead that points toward checkpoint one. Hammer Bot programs: Register 1 — Move 2 to land on the blue conveyor. Registers 2 through 5 continue toward the checkpoint. The key insight? After Register 1, the board elements activate, and that blue conveyor carries Hammer Bot two extra spaces for free. Hammer Bot effectively moved four spaces with one Move 2 card.
walkthrough.image_caption: Blue conveyor belts — two spaces of free movement

walkthrough.title: The Conveyor Bonus Explained
walkthrough.text: Here is why Hammer Bot's play is so clever. After all robots execute their programming for a register, board elements activate. Blue conveyors move robots two spaces. Green conveyors move one. If you can land on a conveyor that points where you want to go, that is free movement without spending a programming card. The best Robo Rally players treat conveyors as part of their route, not just obstacles.

walkthrough.title: Twonky's Program (Aggressive Play)
walkthrough.text: Player 3, Twonky, has a different plan. Twonky sees an energy space and notices that Spin Bot will be in laser range after a couple of moves. Twonky programs: Register 1 — Move 1 toward the energy space. Register 2 — Move 1 to land on it and collect a cube. Then Turn Left in Register 3 to line up a robot laser shot at another player. Registers 4 and 5 continue toward the checkpoint. Twonky is collecting resources while keeping other robots in their crosshairs.

walkthrough.title: Energy and Lasers
walkthrough.text: Twonky's plan combines two key elements. First, landing on an energy space to pick up a free energy cube — that is currency for upgrades later. Second, positioning for robot laser shots. After each register, every robot fires its laser straight ahead. If another robot is in the line of fire without a wall blocking it, they take SPAM damage. Twonky is playing the board AND playing the opponents.

walkthrough.title: Spin Bot's Program (The Mistakes Begin)
walkthrough.text: Now for Player 4, Spin Bot. Spin Bot looks at the nine cards in hand and starts placing them quickly. Move 2 in Register 1 — sure, go forward. Turn Left in Register 2 — that should help. Move 3 in Register 3 — cover lots of ground. Move 1 in Register 4. Turn Right in Register 5. Spin Bot did not trace the path. They just placed cards that seemed good individually. Let's see what happens.

walkthrough.title: Mistake 1: Wrong Card Order
walkthrough.text: Activation Phase begins. Register 1: Spin Bot moves forward 2 spaces. So far so good. Register 2: Turn Left. Wait — now Spin Bot is facing a wall! Register 3: Move 3. Spin Bot slams into the wall after one space and stops dead. Two wasted spaces of movement. Registers 4 and 5 push Spin Bot further into the wrong area of the board, ending up nowhere near the checkpoint. The problem? Spin Bot placed cards without thinking about the ORDER of the full sequence. Each card looked fine alone, but together they sent the robot on a completely wrong path.
walkthrough.image_caption: Five register slots — order matters enormously

walkthrough.title: Lesson 1: Trace Your Full Path
walkthrough.text: Always visualize your full five-card sequence before locking in. Trace your robot's path on the board with your finger first. Card by card, register by register. Where am I after Register 1? Which direction am I facing? What about after Register 2? If you hit a wall at any point, you need to rearrange your cards. A Move 3 is only useful if all three spaces ahead are clear. The five seconds it takes to trace your route saves you an entire round of wasted movement.

walkthrough.title: Round Continues: Zoom Bot Cruises
walkthrough.text: Meanwhile, Zoom Bot executes their clean program perfectly. Move 1, Move 2, Turn Right, Move 1, Move 1 — each register lands exactly where planned. No walls, no surprises. Zoom Bot ends the round within striking distance of checkpoint one. This is what happens when you trace your path first. Boring? Maybe. Effective? Absolutely.

walkthrough.title: Round Continues: Hammer Bot Surfs
walkthrough.text: Hammer Bot's conveyor play pays off beautifully. After Register 1's Move 2 lands them on the blue conveyor, the board elements activate and carry them two bonus spaces toward the checkpoint. Hammer Bot covered four spaces of ground with a single programming card. By the end of the round, Hammer Bot is right at checkpoint one. Free movement is the best movement.

walkthrough.title: Next Round: Spin Bot Programs Again
walkthrough.text: New round. Spin Bot draws nine fresh cards and tries again. This time Spin Bot remembers lesson one — trace the path first. They point at the board and map out five moves. Move 1, Move 2, Turn Right, Move 1, Move 2. That should take them around the wall and toward the checkpoint. The path looks clean. Spin Bot places the cards and feels confident. But there is something Spin Bot forgot to check.

walkthrough.title: Mistake 2: Forgetting Board Elements
walkthrough.text: Activation Phase begins. Registers 1 and 2 go perfectly — Spin Bot moves forward 3 total spaces. But after Register 2, board elements activate. There is a green conveyor belt on the space where Spin Bot landed, and it pushes them one space to the left. Now Spin Bot is one space off from where they expected to be! Register 3's Turn Right now points them in a slightly wrong direction, and the rest of the program sends them off course. Spin Bot traced the robot's path but forgot to account for what the board does AFTER each register.
walkthrough.image_caption: Board elements activate AFTER all robots move each register

walkthrough.title: Lesson 2: Account for Board Elements
walkthrough.text: Board elements activate AFTER all robots move in each register. Conveyors, pushers, and gears can completely change where you end up. Always check what is on the spaces you will land on. When you trace your path, do not just trace your programming cards — also check: is there a conveyor belt here? A gear? A push panel that fires this register? Add those effects to your mental path. Your real position after each register is your programmed move PLUS whatever the board does to you.

walkthrough.title: Twonky Fires Lasers
walkthrough.text: Twonky has been executing their aggressive plan. After positioning in Register 3, Twonky's robot laser fires straight ahead — and Spin Bot is in the line of fire. Spin Bot takes a SPAM damage card. That SPAM card goes into Spin Bot's discard pile and will pollute their programming deck later. Twonky grins. Meanwhile, Twonky collected an energy cube from the energy space back in Register 2. Aggressive play pays off when you plan for it.

walkthrough.title: Next Round: Spin Bot's Third Attempt
walkthrough.text: Another new round. Spin Bot is determined to get this right. This time they carefully trace the path AND check for conveyor belts and gears along the way. The plan looks solid: Move 2, Move 1, Turn Left, Move 2, Move 1 — heading straight for checkpoint one. But Spin Bot notices that Zoom Bot is programming too, and Zoom Bot is closer to the priority antenna. Does that matter? Spin Bot ignores it and locks in.

walkthrough.title: Mistake 3: Ignoring the Priority Antenna
walkthrough.text: Register 3 is where it goes wrong. Spin Bot programmed Turn Left then Move 2 to land on the checkpoint space. But Zoom Bot programmed the same destination space! Since Zoom Bot is closer to the priority antenna, Zoom Bot has higher priority and moves first. Zoom Bot moves onto the checkpoint space before Spin Bot gets there. When Spin Bot tries to move onto that space, they push Zoom Bot forward one space — but Spin Bot ends up on the checkpoint space, not where they actually wanted to be for Registers 4 and 5. The push also sent Zoom Bot in an unintended direction, and now both robots are scrambled.
walkthrough.image_caption: Priority antenna — the robot closest to it moves first each register

walkthrough.title: Lesson 3: Priority Matters
walkthrough.text: Priority matters! The player closest to the priority antenna moves first in each register. If you are far from the antenna, other robots might block your planned path. Before you lock in your program, look at where the other robots are. Think about who moves first. If someone closer to the antenna is heading for the same space, they will get there before you, and you will either push them or get blocked. Factor other robots into your planning, especially in crowded areas near checkpoints.

walkthrough.title: Spin Bot's Redemption
walkthrough.text: One more round. Spin Bot has learned all three lessons the hard way. This time, they go through the full checklist. Step one: trace the path with a finger on the board. Move 1, Turn Right, Move 2, Move 1, Move 1. Step two: check every space for board elements. There is a green conveyor on space three that actually helps — it pushes Spin Bot one space closer to the checkpoint. Step three: check the priority antenna. Spin Bot is closer to it than the other robots heading this direction, so they will move first. Spin Bot locks in with confidence.
walkthrough.image_caption: Five registers programmed with confidence

walkthrough.title: The Clean Run
walkthrough.text: The Activation Phase plays out perfectly. Register 1 — Move 1, right on track. Register 2 — Turn Right, now facing the checkpoint. Register 3 — Move 2, plus the green conveyor gives a bonus space. Register 4 — Move 1, lands on the checkpoint! Spin Bot claims checkpoint one and advances their tracker. Register 5 — Move 1, continuing toward checkpoint two. No walls, no surprises, no collisions. Spin Bot used the conveyor as a tool, moved with priority advantage, and traced every step. That is Robo Rally played right.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You saw Zoom Bot's clean fundamentals, Hammer Bot's conveyor surfing, Twonky's aggressive laser play, and Spin Bot's journey from chaos to mastery. Remember Spin Bot's three hard-earned lessons: always trace your full five-card path before locking in. Always account for board elements that activate after your moves. And always check who has priority before planning your route through traffic. You are ready to race.

walkthrough.title: Before You Race
walkthrough.text:
Before you hit the factory floor for real, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategy basics and common mistakes to avoid.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to programming cards, board elements, and damage types.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Now get out there and race!

summary.title: Before You Race
bullets: Check out General Tips → strategy basics for beginners
Reference the Appendix → programming cards, board elements, damage types
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Always Trace Your Full Path
walkthrough.text: This is the number one tip for new Robo Rally players, and it is worth repeating until it becomes second nature. Before you place a single card on your registers, point at the board and trace your robot's full five-move path. After Register 1, where are you? Which direction are you facing? After Register 2? Register 3? All the way through Register 5. Check every space for walls. If your Move 2 would hit a wall after one space, you only move one space and waste the rest. Five seconds of tracing saves entire rounds of wasted movement. Do this every single time you program.

walkthrough.title: Account for Board Elements in Your Planning
walkthrough.text: Tracing your programming cards is only half the picture. After each register, board elements activate: blue conveyors move you two spaces, green conveyors move you one space, gears rotate you, and push panels shove you during specific registers. When you trace your path, add these effects after each register. Your real position is your programmed move PLUS whatever the board does to you. A perfectly planned path can go completely wrong if you forget about a conveyor belt that pushes you sideways after Register 2.

walkthrough.title: Understand Priority — Who Moves First Matters
walkthrough.text: The robot closest to the antenna gets highest priority. This matters because if two robots are heading for the same space, whoever has priority gets there first and the other robot either pushes them or gets blocked. Before you lock in your program, look at where other robots are and who is closest to the antenna. If someone with higher priority is heading your way, you might collide. Adjust your path to avoid traffic, or plan to use the push to your advantage.

walkthrough.title: Energy Management — Power Up vs Move Forward
walkthrough.text: Power Up cards let your robot stand still while collecting one energy cube. Energy buys upgrades, and upgrades can be game-changing. New players often skip Power Up because it feels like wasting a turn, but building up five or six energy lets you buy a powerful permanent upgrade that helps you every round for the rest of the game. The key is timing. Do not Power Up when you are one space away from a checkpoint. Do Power Up when you are in a safe position with no immediate path to your next goal. Think of energy as an investment in your robot's future performance.

walkthrough.title: Do Not Panic About Damage Cards
walkthrough.text: Getting hit is normal in Robo Rally. Lasers, collisions, and hazards will give you SPAM cards that pollute your programming deck. One or two SPAM cards in your deck are manageable — you draw nine cards and only need five good ones. Do not waste moves trying to avoid every single laser. Focus on reaching checkpoints efficiently and treat minor damage as the cost of doing business. The real danger is accumulating four or five SPAM cards, which severely limits your programming options. Look for chances to use the Spam Folder card to clean up your deck when damage starts piling up.

walkthrough.title: Checkpoints Must Be Reached in Order
walkthrough.text: A common beginner mistake is racing to whichever checkpoint looks closest on the board. You must claim checkpoints in numerical order — checkpoint one before two, two before three, and so on. Landing on checkpoint three when you have not claimed checkpoint one does nothing. Before you plan your route, check your player mat to see which checkpoint you need next, then plan your path to that specific flag. Skipping ahead wastes rounds and lets other players pull ahead in the race.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Here are the biggest beginner traps, all in one place. First, programming without tracing — this leads to running into walls and wasting entire rounds. Second, ignoring conveyor belts — they activate after your move and can push you off your planned path. Third, forgetting priority — if someone closer to the antenna is heading for the same space, they get there first. Fourth, hoarding energy without ever buying upgrades — energy in your supply scores zero points, but upgrades can win you the race. Fifth, panicking when the sand timer flips — have a rough plan before drawing cards so you can program quickly when pressured.

walkthrough.title: Use the Safest Path, Not the Shortest
walkthrough.text: The shortest route to your next checkpoint might run through a gauntlet of lasers, past a pit, and through a crowd of other robots. Every reboot costs you two SPAM cards AND the rest of your round — that is a massive setback. Sometimes the route that takes one extra round but avoids pits and heavy traffic gets you there faster overall. Look for paths that steer clear of board edges, pits, and congested areas. A safe arrival beats a dramatic crash every time. Save the risky plays for when you are behind and need to catch up.

walkthrough.title: Read the Board Before You Draw Cards
walkthrough.text: Before you even draw your nine programming cards, spend a few seconds studying the board. Where is your next checkpoint? What board elements are between you and it? Where are the other robots? Who has priority? Once you have a rough plan — go forward two, turn right, go forward three — then draw your cards and look for the pieces that fit. Players who plan before drawing program faster and make fewer mistakes. Players who draw first and plan second are the ones scrambling when the sand timer flips.

walkthrough.title: Have Fun with the Chaos
walkthrough.text: Here is the most important tip of all: Robo Rally is meant to be chaotic and hilarious. Your carefully planned path WILL fall apart. Another robot WILL push you into a pit. A SPAM card WILL replace your perfect Move 3 with a random draw. That is not the game breaking — that IS the game. The best players laugh when their robot gets shoved off course, adapt on the fly, and come up with a new plan. Do not get frustrated by chaos. Embrace it. The funniest moments come from the biggest disasters, and those disasters make the eventual victories even sweeter.

Five seconds of tracing saves entire rounds of bad programming

summary.title: Do Not Panic About Damage

Focus on checkpoints, treat minor damage as cost of business
Use Spam Folder to clean up when damage piles up

summary.title: Checkpoints Must Be in Order

Landing on a future checkpoint before claiming previous ones does nothing
Always check your tracker to know which checkpoint is next

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Programming without tracing your path first
Ignoring conveyors and gears that activate after your move
Forgetting priority — who moves first matters in crowded areas
Panicking when the sand timer flips

summary.title: Use the Safest Path
bullets: Rebooting costs 2 SPAM + rest of your round — huge setback
Avoid pits, board edges, and heavy traffic when possible
One extra round of safe travel beats a dramatic crash

summary.title: Read the Board Before Drawing Cards
bullets: Study the board and form a rough plan BEFORE drawing 9 cards
Know your target direction, then find matching cards
Planning first = faster programming when the timer hits

summary.title: Have Fun with the Chaos
bullets: Plans will fall apart — that IS the game, not a bug
Laugh at disasters, adapt on the fly, make new plans
The funniest moments come from the biggest crashes

walkthrough.title: Conveyor Surfing
walkthrough.text: Advanced players do not just avoid conveyor belts — they ride them. Conveyor surfing means intentionally programming your robot to land on conveyor belts that point toward your objective. A blue conveyor gives you two free spaces of movement, and a green gives you one. If you can chain two blue conveyors together, that is four bonus spaces in a single register. Plan your programming cards so that your move ends on a conveyor, let the board carry you, then program your next move from the new position. On courses like Dizzy Highway and Twister, conveyor surfing can shave entire rounds off your race time.

walkthrough.title: Laser Positioning
walkthrough.text: Your robot fires its laser straight ahead after every register. Good players position their robot to get free shots at opponents across multiple registers. The ideal laser position is a long, clear corridor with no walls between you and a traffic lane where other robots are likely to pass. If you can line up your robot facing down a corridor at the start of a round, you might hit opponents in two or three consecutive registers without moving. Each hit is a SPAM card that degrades their programming deck. Over several rounds, consistent laser pressure makes opponents noticeably weaker.

walkthrough.title: Upgrade Card Tier List
walkthrough.text: Not all upgrades are created equal. The top tier permanent upgrades are ones that give you extra movement options, damage protection, or deck improvement every single round. Movement upgrades like Rear Laser or Brakes let you play more aggressively without as much risk. Damage-reduction upgrades keep your deck clean and your programming options wide. Mid tier upgrades are situationally powerful, like ones that interact with specific board elements. Low tier upgrades are temporary one-shot effects that rarely justify their energy cost. When the shop refreshes, identify the tier first and only spend energy on top or strong mid tier cards.

walkthrough.title: Damage Management
walkthrough.text: At the advanced level, damage is not just something that happens to you — it is something you manage strategically. Sometimes taking one SPAM card to reach a checkpoint this round is better than spending two extra rounds on a safe route. The decision framework is simple: will this damage cost me more time than the shortcut saves? One or two SPAM cards are easily absorbed. Three or four start limiting your options. Five or more means your programming phase becomes a desperate scramble. Use Spam Folder aggressively to keep your deck under three SPAM cards. If an upgrade offers damage removal, it is almost always worth buying.

walkthrough.title: Blocking Strategies
walkthrough.text: Your robot is a physical obstacle that other robots must deal with. Parking on a checkpoint forces every opponent to push you off before they can claim it — and pushing you means they end up one space past the checkpoint, wasting their programming. Blocking a narrow corridor prevents opponents from using an optimal path. You can even use your robot as a wall to redirect conveyor belt traffic — if a robot would be pushed onto your space by a conveyor, the push is blocked. Advanced players think about where their robot's body physically disrupts opponent routes, especially in the late game when everyone is converging on the same checkpoints.

walkthrough.title: Checkpoint Rush vs Energy Farming
walkthrough.text: Two viable race strategies exist: checkpoint rushing and energy farming. Rushers program maximum movement every round, skipping Power Up cards and energy spaces to reach checkpoints as fast as possible. They win by speed and accept a messy, SPAM-filled deck. Farmers spend the first two or three rounds collecting energy and buying powerful upgrades, then use those upgrades to blitz through checkpoints faster in the mid and late game. The best choice depends on the course. Short courses with two or three checkpoints favor rushing. Long courses with five or six checkpoints favor farming because upgrades compound over more rounds.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Board
walkthrough.text: Every Robo Rally course has optimal paths hidden in its layout. Reading the board means identifying the fastest route through hazards by combining programmed movement with board elements. Look for conveyor chains that carry you multiple spaces toward checkpoints. Identify safe lanes with no lasers and no pit adjacency. Find gear spaces that give you a free turn in the direction you need. Map out push panel timings — which registers do they fire, and can you use them for free movement? The best players spend the first round studying the board layout as much as they study their cards. Once you see the optimal paths, you can program them round after round.

walkthrough.title: Multi-Register Planning
walkthrough.text: Beginner planning stops at where am I after each register. Advanced planning considers the cascading effects across all five registers plus board element interactions. For example: if Register 1's Move 2 lands you on a blue conveyor, after board elements you are two spaces further. That means Register 2's Move 1 starts from a position three spaces ahead of where you began. A gear in Register 3's landing space rotates you, saving a Turn card for Register 4. This cascading awareness — programming plus board plus programming plus board — is what separates advanced players from beginners. Think of your five registers as one continuous sequence with board element bonuses woven in, not five separate moves.

walkthrough.title: Priority Manipulation
walkthrough.text: At the advanced level, you can manipulate your priority intentionally. If you want to push an opponent off a checkpoint, program a path that moves you closer to the priority antenna before the critical register, guaranteeing you move first. If you want to avoid collisions, stay far from the antenna so you move last and can see where everyone ends up. Some players even sacrifice one register of forward progress to reposition relative to the antenna for a crucial push play in the next register. Priority is not random — it is a tool you can control through positioning.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Tactics
walkthrough.text: The final checkpoint approach requires different thinking than the rest of the race. Everyone converges on the same small area, creating maximum chaos. Advanced endgame tactics include: programming to arrive at the checkpoint in Register 5, which is harder to block because opponents have already moved. Using Again cards to repeat a successful move from the previous register for guaranteed positioning. Intentionally pushing the leader away from the checkpoint while positioning yourself to claim it. And sacrificing your body — rebooting on purpose near the final checkpoint because the reboot token is closer to the goal than your current position. The endgame is where knowledge of pushing, priority, and board elements all combine for the final sprint.

bullets: Intentionally land on conveyors pointing toward your objective
Blue = 2 free spaces, green = 1 — chain them for massive bonus movement
Plan your Move card to end on a conveyor, then program from the new position

summary.title: Laser Positioning
bullets: Position robot facing down corridors where opponents will pass
Free SPAM damage across 2-3 consecutive registers without moving
Consistent laser pressure degrades opponent decks over time

summary.title: Upgrade Card Tier List
bullets: Top tier: extra movement, damage protection, deck improvement (permanent)
Mid tier: situationally powerful board element interactions
Low tier: temporary one-shot effects rarely worth the energy cost

summary.title: Damage Management
bullets: 1-2 SPAM: easily absorbed. 3-4: limiting. 5+: desperate scramble
Taking damage for a shortcut can be worth it if it saves a full round
Use Spam Folder aggressively — keep deck under 3 SPAM cards

summary.title: Blocking Strategies
bullets: Park on checkpoints to force opponents to push you off first
Block narrow corridors to deny optimal routes
Your robot body blocks conveyor pushes — use it to redirect traffic

summary.title: Checkpoint Rush vs Energy Farming
bullets: Rush: max movement, skip Power Up, win by raw speed
Farm: collect energy, buy upgrades, blitz checkpoints later
Short courses (2-3 flags) favor rush; long courses (5-6) favor farming

summary.title: Reading the Board
bullets: Identify conveyor chains, safe lanes, and useful gear rotations
Map push panel timing — which registers they fire
Spend Round 1 studying the board layout as much as your cards

summary.title: Multi-Register Planning
bullets: Think of 5 registers as one continuous sequence with board bonuses woven in
Each register starts from post-board-element position, not just post-card
Cascading: Move + conveyor + Move + gear + Move = far more ground than 3 cards alone

summary.title: Priority Manipulation
bullets: Move closer to antenna before critical registers to guarantee moving first
Stay far from antenna when you want predictable, collision-free movement
Sacrifice one register of progress to reposition for a crucial push play

summary.title: Endgame Tactics
bullets: Arrive at final checkpoint in Register 5 — hardest to block
Use Again cards for guaranteed repeat positioning
Push the leader away while claiming the checkpoint yourself
Consider rebooting near a closer reboot token as a shortcut

entries.definition: Programming card. Move your robot one space forward in the direction it is currently facing. If it hits another robot, push that robot one space in the same direction.
Programming card. Move your robot two spaces forward in the direction it is currently facing. Each space can trigger a push if another robot is in the way.
Programming card. Move your robot three spaces forward in the direction it is currently facing. Each space can trigger a push. Great for covering ground quickly but risky on crowded boards.
Programming card. Move your robot one space backward without changing the direction it faces. Useful for backing away from hazards while keeping your orientation.
Programming card. Rotate your robot ninety degrees to the left. The robot stays in its current space. Does not trigger any movement or pushing.
Programming card. Rotate your robot ninety degrees to the right. The robot stays in its current space. Does not trigger any movement or pushing.
Programming card. Rotate your robot one hundred eighty degrees so it faces the opposite direction. The robot stays in its current space.
Programming card. Your robot stays in place and you collect one energy cube from the bank. Energy is used to purchase upgrade cards during the Upgrade Phase.

Programming card. Repeat the programming action from the previous register. If Again is placed in register one, it does nothing because there is no previous register. If the previous register was a damage card, Again copies the replacement action that was drawn.
Special programming card. Take one energy cube and place it on your player mat. Available through certain game effects, not part of your standard programming deck.
Special programming card. When activated, choose one of: Turn Left, Turn Right, or U-Turn. Gives you flexible turning options in a single card.
Special programming card. Permanently discard one SPAM damage card from your discard pile back to the SPAM damage draw pile. Helps clean your deck of damage pollution.
Special programming card. When activated, choose any standard programming action: Move one, Move two, Move three, Back Up, Turn Left, Turn Right, or U-Turn. Maximum flexibility.
Special programming card. Move your robot three spaces in the direction it is facing. Functions like a Move three card.
Special programming card. Repeats the programming from the previous register, similar to the Again card.

Board element. Activates first in the board element sequence. Moves any robot standing on it two spaces in the direction of the arrows. If the belt curves, the robot turns with it. Robots can chain across multiple belt segments.
Board element. Activates second in the board element sequence. Moves any robot standing on it one space in the direction of the arrow. Slower than blue conveyors but still provides free movement.
Board element. Activates third in the sequence. Only fires during specific numbered registers shown on the panel. When active, pushes any robot on it one space in the direction the panel faces.
Board element. Activates fourth in the sequence. Rotates any robot standing on it ninety degrees. Clockwise gears turn the robot to the right, counterclockwise gears turn it to the left.

Board element. Activates fifth in the sequence. Fires a beam from a wall-mounted emitter in a straight line. The first robot in the beam's path takes one SPAM damage card. Lasers are blocked by walls and other robots.

Board element. Activates sixth in the sequence. Each robot fires a laser beam straight ahead in the direction it faces. The first robot in the line of fire takes one SPAM damage card. Blocked by walls and robots.

Board element. Activates seventh in the sequence. If a robot is on an energy space with a cube on it, the robot collects that cube. On register five, collect one energy from the bank even if the space is empty.

Board element. Activates last in the sequence. If a robot is on a checkpoint and it is the next in their numerical sequence, they claim it and advance their tracker. Must be claimed in order: one, two, three, etc.
Board hazard. If a robot enters a pit space for any reason (movement, push, conveyor), it immediately reboots. Pits are permanent and cannot be blocked or removed.
Board feature. Printed on the edge of spaces. Blocks robot movement, pushes, and laser fire from the side the wall is on. Robots cannot move or be pushed through walls.

The most common damage type. Goes into your discard pile and pollutes your programming deck. When drawn during Programming Phase and placed on a register, discard it on activation and draw a random replacement card from your deck.
When programmed into a register and activated, your robot immediately reboots. You take two SPAM cards, discard remaining programming, and respawn on the nearest reboot token.
When programmed into a register and activated, you immediately take two additional SPAM damage cards on top of discarding the Trojan Horse. Doubles your damage in one register.

The most dangerous damage type. When programmed into a register and activated, every robot within a six space radius of your robot must also take a Virus card. Spreads infectiously across the factory floor.
Upgrade cards with a yellow border. Once installed, they remain active for the entire game. You can have up to three permanent upgrades installed at once. Discard one to make room if at maximum.
Upgrade cards with a red border. Single-use abilities that are discarded after activation. You can have up to three temporary upgrades installed at once.

One of five numbered slots on your player mat where you place programming cards. Registers are resolved in order from one through five during the Activation Phase. Each register is a single programmed action for your robot.

Turn order within each register, determined by distance to the priority antenna. The robot closest to the antenna has highest priority and executes its programming first. Ties broken by clockwise sweep from the antenna dish.
A token placed on a fixed board space. Determines the order robots execute their programming each register. Count shortest path (rows and columns, not diagonals) from each robot to the antenna.

When a robot is destroyed (pit, off-board, Worm card), it reboots. Take two SPAM cards, discard remaining programming, place on nearest reboot token facing any direction. Skip remaining registers this round.
Translucent orange cubes used as currency. Gained from Power Up cards, energy spaces, and certain effects. Spent during the Upgrade Phase to purchase upgrade cards from the shop.
Each player's personal deck of twenty cards containing movement, turning, Power Up, and Again cards. Damage cards get mixed into this deck over time, reducing the quality of your draws.
Face-up upgrade cards equal to the player count, refreshed each round. Players may purchase one upgrade per round during the Upgrade Phase, in priority order.
Your robot's command center. Contains five register slots for programming, Draw and Discard pile areas, energy tracker (zero to ten), checkpoint tracker, and slots for installed upgrades.

A thirty-second timer. Once the first player finishes programming all five registers, the timer is flipped. Any player who has not finished programming when the timer runs out has their remaining empty registers filled with random cards from their deck.
When a robot moves into a space occupied by another robot, the stationary robot is pushed one space in the direction of movement. Pushes can chain through multiple robots. Blocked by walls.

After each register, board elements activate in this fixed order: one, blue conveyor belts. Two, green conveyor belts. Three, push panels. Four, gears. Five, board lasers. Six, robot lasers. Seven, energy spaces. Eight, checkpoints.
